extra.reasoning: The property requires NOT (rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b). Only r5 (priority 50) can set rec_drug_a := true, and its condition is (g6pd_deficiency AND seizure_history AND heart_failure AND hepatic_impairment). Only r3 (priority 50) can set rec_drug_b := true, and its condition is g6pd_deficiency. Crucially, r5's guard includes g6pd_deficiency as a conjunct, so whenever r5 fires (rec_drug_a = true), g6pd_deficiency must be true, which necessarily fires r3 (rec_drug_b = true). The two rules target different outputs, so priority/tie-breaking never suppresses either one, and defaults only apply to unset outputs. Therefore any input satisfying all four conjuncts of r5 makes both outputs true. Concrete counterexample: g6pd_deficiency=true, seizure_history=true, heart_failure=true, hepatic_impairment=true (bradycardia, immunosuppressed, anticoagulated all false). Trace: r3 -> rec_drug_b=true; r5 -> rec_drug_a=true; rec_drug_c stays false by default. Result: rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b both true, violating mutual exclusion.
